limitations: Analysis is based on summarized operational evidence and retained calibration snapshots.
The morning transition hypothesis is directional only because the provided facts do not explicitly identify the business-day state change.

executiveSummary.headline: Model outperformed TEPCO overall, but midday shape errors remain material
executiveSummary.summary: The model achieved lower daily error than TEPCO on 16 of 24 hours, with MAE 453.9 MW vs 561.7 MW and WAPE 1.65% vs 2.04%. However, the 11-15 band underperformed TEPCO, and the largest miss occurred at hour 15 with a +1612.8 MW error after a sharp shape reversal around 14-15.

rootCauseHypotheses.title: Midday ramp reversal was not captured
rootCauseHypotheses.explanation: The largest shape break was 14->15: actual demand fell 970.0 MW while the model rose 1195.9 MW. This produced the day’s worst model miss at hour 15 and a large delta error of 2165.9 MW.

rootCauseHypotheses.title: Daytime underprediction persisted through 11-14
rootCauseHypotheses.explanation: The model was below actual demand at hours 11-14, including -1524.4 MW at hour 13 and -553.1 MW at hour 14. This shows the daytime level was too low before the hour-15 overshoot.
counterEvidence: TEPCO also missed hour 13, so this is not a model-only daytime level issue.

rootCauseHypotheses.title: Morning ramp bias suggests transition contamination
rootCauseHypotheses.explanation: In 06-10, the model had MAE 617.0 MW and a negative bias of -397.4 MW, while the business-type diagnostics show a strong 06-10 lag24 same-type gap and recent same-type delta. This is consistent with transition or lag contamination, especially if the day included a business-day to non-business-day regime change, but that transition is not directly observed here.
counterEvidence: The business-day to non-business-day transition itself is not explicitly recorded in the provided facts.

featureRecommendations.suggestion: Activate damping when the 2-hour forecast slope turns positive while actual demand is falling, using a factor cap of 0.70 for hours 14-16.
featureRecommendations.expectedEffect: Reduce overshoot on rapid midday reversals and lower hour-15 absolute error.
featureRecommendations.risk: Too much damping could flatten legitimate late-afternoon rebounds.
featureRecommendations.validationPlan: Replay 2026-05-25 and require lower 14-15 delta error versus the current 2165.9 MW break while preserving 11-14 level accuracy.

featureRecommendations.suggestion: Apply a daytime scale-up trigger when 11-14 cumulative residual stays below -400 MW for two consecutive hours.
featureRecommendations.expectedEffect: Lift daytime level before the hour-15 reversal and cut the 13-14 underprediction.
featureRecommendations.risk: Could overcorrect on already elevated demand days.
featureRecommendations.validationPlan: Replay days with 11-14 residuals below -400 MW and check MAE reduction in the 11-15 band without increasing overnight error.

operatorNotes: Daily comparable coverage was complete at 24/24 hours, with 22 observed hours used in calibration and 19 retained calibration snapshots.
Published-vs-recalculated gaps exceeded the 500 MW freeze threshold at hours 14 and 15, but the published line still reflects a frozen serving path rather than a calibration fault.
TEPCO remained better in the 11-15 band, while the model retained clear advantage overnight, late afternoon, and evening.
limitations: Analysis is based on summarized operational evidence and retained calibration snapshots.
The morning transition hypothesis is directional only because the provided facts do not explicitly identify the business-day state change.
